summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Feeling sick for several days, with a runny nose and stuffy nose.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports feeling sick for several days, starting around supper time last night with a runny nose and stuffy nose. He has experienced persistent mucous, which is clear. He also reports a cough, which is not barky. He has been pulling on his ears and reports some ear pain. He has been rubbing his nose frequently. He reports a rectal temperature of 100.3°F. He has been using saline mist and Baby Vicks on his feet. He has been using Aveno shampoo and cradle cap brush on his cradle cap. He reports decreased appetite and irritability. He has been taking Tylenol this morning. He consumed approximately seven ounces from a bottle this morning and barely ate his breakfast. He has been sweating. He reports feeling warm and dizziness. He has been using saline mist and Tylenol.

Findings
Examination reveals clear mucous. The patient reports pulling on his ears and experiencing ear pain. The patient is rubbing his nose frequently. The patient's rectal temperature was 100.3°F. The patient is sweating.

Assessment
The patient likely has a viral infection, possibly a sinus infection. Given the symptoms, a bacterial infection cannot be ruled out at this time.

Plan
Continue saline mist and Tylenol as needed. Follow up in one week if symptoms do not improve or if a barky cough develops. If a barky cough develops, administer half a teaspoon of sister's medicine. Order shoulder radiographs and initiate physical therapy. Arrange follow-up in four weeks if symptoms persist. Give the patient half a teaspoon of ibuprofen. Give the patient agave as an alternative to honey. Give the patient a soft brush and baby oil. Check out in six weeks.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Randy Gutierrez is a 9-month-old male who is being evaluated today for a cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea. He is accompanied by his mother.

Symptoms began yesterday around dinnertime with clear nasal drainage, pulling on his ears bilaterally, and rubbing his nose frequently. He does not have a barky cough or a sore throat. His rectal temperature was 100.3 degrees. The patient did consume a 7 ounce bottle this morning, but mostly denied the next offered bottle and declined cereal. He is irritable and having trouble falling asleep, described “like he is fighting it”.

The patient has been exposed to an older sister who is sick and a cousin who has a sinus infection.

Treatment has included saline nasal spray, Tylenol, baby Vick's, and a humidifier. When the patient aroused this morning, he was warm to the touch. When his sleeper was removed, he was quite sweaty despite the air conditioner being on.

The patient's dandruff is also worsening. The only tried treatment is Aveeno shampoo.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Older sister.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Tylenol PRN.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Temperature 100.3 degrees.

Ears, Eyes, Nose, Throat
Nasal congestion, clear mucus, rhinorrhea. Bilateral pulling of ears, no sore throat or hoarseness.

Respiratory
Positive for cough.

ASSESSMENT

• Viral infection
• Dandruff

PLAN

Viral infection
The patient presents with a viral infection and does not need antibiotics currently. I advised continuation of fluids, rest. Additionally, I suggested trying Zarbee’s for children under 1 years old. If a croup cough develops, the patient will receive a 0.5 teaspoon.

I also provided counseling to the mother to avoid giving honey to the patient for his first year of life due to botulism spores.

Dandruff
The patient is experiencing worsening symptoms of dandruff. I recommended Head and Shoulders shampoo, Selsun Blue, or Nizoral A-D 2 times per week, combined with his usual shampoo. I counseled the mother that she should be careful not to get the shampoo in the patient eyes and to not use the extra strength shampoos. Additionally, he may also use baby oil and a soft brush on the area.

INSTRUCTIONS

Return in one week for a re-check.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports pulling on his ears and experiencing ear pain.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a patient-reported symptom, not an objective exam finding, and belongs in the symptom history rather than the exam section.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient is rubbing his nose frequently.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is primarily a behavior described in the history; while it could be observed on exam, it is presented as a patient behavior rather than a discrete physical finding.
Sweating is described as part of the symptom complex in the history; unless explicitly documented as an observed sign, it fits better in HPI.

section_miscategorizations.text: Order shoulder radiographs and initiate physical therapy.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Content is plan-type but is clearly unrelated to the presenting URI/ear/nasal complaint and appears to be an erroneously inserted plan for a different problem, which could mislead a reader about the current visit's focus.

section_miscategorizations.text: Arrange follow-up in four weeks if symptoms persist.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This follow-up timing conflicts with other follow-up instructions in the same plan (one week and six weeks), suggesting misplacement or carryover from another context, though it remains plan-type content.

This follow-up interval is inconsistent with the acute nature of the complaint and other follow-up instructions, suggesting it may belong to a different encounter, but it is still formally a plan element.
